behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is cross-module handoff: source collectors -> aggregator -> summary-generation input. A unit test of one function could miss wiring/omission bugs where one source is fetched but dropped before handoff. Real OS/network is not required because source clients can be in-process fakes; correctness does not depend on model output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Run the report pipeline with three stub source adapters returning distinct sentinel payloads and a spy/fake summary generator. Assert the exact payload passed to summary generation contains all three source sections and source-identifying values (none missing/overwritten).

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a `data unavailable` note for that section rather than aborting.
behaviors.justification: Behavior targets resilience across module boundaries (fetch error propagation into report composition). Unit scope is too low because real failure is often in orchestration between fetch, error handling, and rendering layers. System is not required: unavailability can be represented by a stub adapter raising timeout/HTTP errors in-process. No dependence on AI output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real orchestration/composition modules with two healthy stub sources and one failing stub source (exception/timeout). Assert run completes, output report contains a `data unavailable` marker only for the failed section, and remaining sections are populated.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on the model’s generated content quality (faithfulness to input values). A mocked or deterministic stub summary would always hide hallucination/omission/paraphrase errors. Rejected higher level Workflow because behavior text describes a single generation quality check, not multiple agentic decisions in sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Feed representative report inputs with known key metrics/issues/deploy facts to the real summary model and evaluate output for factual grounding (required values present, no contradictory values). Run multiple trials and fail on unacceptable factual error rate.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary includes real external infrastructure: end-to-end collection + outbound email + inbox receipt. In-process mocks can hide SMTP/API auth, transport, formatting, and delivery-path failures. Rejected Agentic as minimum because behavior text focuses on delivery to inbox, not judging LLM output quality; model content can be fixed/controlled while still validating end-to-end transport.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the script as a real process in a staging environment with reachable source endpoints and real email service/test mailbox. Assert one run produces an email in the configured inbox with expected subject/body structure and included report sections.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run.
behaviors.justification: Behavior spans persistence across runs and external delivery failure conditions. Real failure often appears at process/OS boundary (file persistence/readback between executions) and real delivery connectivity behavior. Integration fakes may miss restart/state and I/O edge cases. No AI-quality dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run 1 (email endpoint unreachable/refused): execute script, assert fallback report file is created on disk with expected content/metadata. Run 2 (endpoint reachable): execute next scheduled invocation as a separate process, assert previous report is retried/sent and fallback state is cleared or marked delivered.
